mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Singapore on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia, in the south-east. Singapore is a tiny island country at the very tip of the Malay Peninsula, 137 km north of the equator 🌍

mapQuestions.question: How big is Singapore?
mapQuestions.answer: About 280 square miles, smaller than the city of Phoenix and not much bigger than the Isle of Wight. But it packs in about 6 million people. One of the most densely populated countries on Earth.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Singapore speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Singapore has FOUR official languages: English, Mandarin Chinese, Malay (the national language) and Tamil. Most Singaporeans speak two or three of these, sometimes mixing them in one sentence ("Singlish").

mapQuestions.question: How did Singapore become its own country?
mapQuestions.answer: Singapore was kicked out of Malaysia in 1965 after only two years of being part of it, mostly because of political disagreements. The country was tiny, broke and had almost no resources. In 50 years it became one of the richest countries in the world.

mapQuestions.question: Why are there so many trees in Singapore?
mapQuestions.answer: Because the government deliberately made it that way. After independence, Lee Kuan Yew (the first prime minister) started a city-wide tree-planting programme. Singapore now calls itself "a city in a garden". There are over 2 million trees in the city.

foods.name: Hainanese chicken rice
foods.description: Poached chicken with rice cooked in chicken stock, garlic and pandan, served with cucumber and three dipping sauces (chilli, ginger and dark soy). Singapore's unofficial national dish.

foods.name: Chilli crab
foods.description: Whole mud crab smashed and stir-fried in a sweet-spicy tomato-chilli-egg sauce. Eat with your hands and sweep up the leftover sauce with deep-fried "mantou" buns.

foods.name: Laksa
foods.description: A rich coconut-curry noodle soup with prawns, fish cake and bean sprouts. The Singaporean (and Malaysian) hawker centre classic.

foods.name: Satay
foods.description: Marinated chicken, beef or mutton on bamboo skewers grilled over charcoal, served with thick spicy peanut sauce, rice cakes, cucumber and onion.

foods.name: Kaya toast
foods.description: Two slices of toast filled with sweet coconut-egg jam ("kaya") and a thick slab of butter, eaten with soft boiled eggs and dark "kopi-O" coffee. The classic Singapore breakfast.

foods.name: Char kway teow
foods.description: Stir-fried flat rice noodles in dark soy, with prawns, Chinese sausage, beansprouts and egg, cooked over a screaming-hot wok flame.

scavengerItems.label: The Marina Bay Sands "boat on three towers" hotel
The Supertree Grove vertical-garden towers at Gardens by the Bay
The Merlion statue (half lion, half fish) spitting water into Marina Bay
A "hawker centre" with dozens of food stalls and packed shared tables
The spotless MRT subway with arrival times on every screen
A "tissue packet" reserving a hawker table (the Singaporean way to claim a seat)
A Chinese, Hindu and Muslim place of worship within walking distance of each other (Little India, Chinatown, Arab Street)
A short, drenching tropical rainstorm that ends in 20 minutes
A monitor lizard or otter family in the Botanic Gardens or river
"Singapore Sling" being made at Raffles Hotel (it's a fruit-cocktail mocktail-able)
Sentosa Island's beaches and Universal Studios theme park
The MacRitchie treetop walk through Singapore's small remaining rainforest

geoMatches.description: The famous skyline waterfront. Marina Bay Sands hotel, the Gardens by the Bay supertrees, the ArtScience Museum (lotus-shaped), and the Merlion statue.
A neighbourhood of red lanterns, shophouses, hawker centres and temples (including the Buddha Tooth Relic Temple).
Colourful streets full of saree shops, garland sellers, dosa restaurants and the Sri Veeramakaliamman Hindu temple.
A small leisure island just south of the main city, with beaches, theme parks, an aquarium, and a cable car running to it.
A UNESCO World Heritage tropical garden right in the city, with thousands of orchid varieties in the National Orchid Garden.
Singapore's airport, regularly ranked the world's best. Has an indoor waterfall (the Rain Vortex), butterfly garden, free movie theatre and rooftop swimming pool.

senses.smell: Wok-fired oil and garlic in a hawker centre, durian fruit (the famous smelly tropical fruit, banned on the MRT!), warm tropical rain on hot pavement, jasmine garlands in Little India, kaya-toast butter melting…
senses.hear: "Selamat pagi!" "Ni hao!" "Vanakkam!" all in the same lift, MRT announcements in four languages, hawker stall sizzle, tropical rain hammering on roofs, evening cicadas in Botanic Gardens…
senses.taste: Tender Hainanese chicken with ginger sauce, fiery chilli crab with mantou bread, sweet-savoury kaya toast, spicy laksa noodles, ice-cold sugarcane juice on a sticky day…
senses.feel: Soupy tropical humidity (Singapore is hot ALL year), the cold blast of air-con as you walk into a mall, smooth Sentosa beach sand, the steam of a hawker bowl in your face…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How did Singapore become a country by accident?
stories.body: In 1963 Singapore joined the Federation of Malaysia, hoping that being part of a bigger country would give it security and trade. But the politicians in Kuala Lumpur and the politicians in Singapore couldn't agree on almost anything, and on 9 August 1965 Singapore was VOTED OUT of Malaysia and forced to become its own country. Singapore's leader, Lee Kuan Yew, gave a famous press conference where he cried on television about being cast adrift. The new country had no army, no oil, no farmland, no fresh water, just 1.9 million people on a small island. Sixty years later, Singapore has one of the highest standards of living on Earth, with universal healthcare, top-tier schools, and a port that handles more containers than almost anywhere else in the world.

stories.question: What is "Singlish"?
stories.body: Singlish is a creative spoken English that mixes English with Malay, Hokkien Chinese, Tamil and Cantonese words and grammar, all in the same sentence. "Can or not?" means "is that OK?". "Don't pray pray" means "don't mess about". "Lah" is a softener at the end of sentences (no real meaning, just texture). "Shiok!" means "delicious, brilliant, perfect". The Singaporean government has sometimes tried to discourage Singlish in favour of "proper" English, but Singlish is one of the most loved parts of Singaporean identity. There is even a Singlish dictionary.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A country invented its own version of English by mixing in other languages. Does that feel like a strength of English or a weakness?

stories.question: How is Singapore a "city in a garden"?
stories.body: In 1967, just two years after independence, Singapore's first prime minister Lee Kuan Yew launched a "garden city" plan. He thought green spaces would calm a tightly packed island city and make it pleasant to live in. Today Singapore has over 2 million trees, parks within 10 minutes of every home, and entire skyscrapers covered in plants. Gardens by the Bay (opened in 2012) has 18 huge "supertrees", 25 to 50 metres tall, that double as vertical gardens and solar power collectors. They light up at night. Singapore's parliament has even legislated that nearly all new buildings must include green walls or roof gardens. The whole island is a kind of city-scale experiment in living with nature.

stories.question: What is a hawker centre?
stories.body: A hawker centre is an open-air food court with dozens of small stalls, each one specialising in just one dish: chicken rice, laksa, char kway teow, satay, dim sum, Indian dosa. Hawker centres started in the 1970s when the Singaporean government cleaned up the street-food vendors of the colonial era and gave them small permanent stalls in covered communal spaces. Today hawker centres are where most Singaporeans eat most of their meals. A bowl of laksa might cost SGD 5 (about £3). UNESCO put Singaporean hawker culture on its list of "intangible cultural heritage" in 2020.

stories.question: Why is Changi Airport so famous?
stories.body: Changi is regularly voted the best airport in the world. It has an INDOOR rainforest waterfall (the Jewel Rain Vortex, 40 metres tall, the biggest indoor waterfall on Earth), a butterfly garden, a rooftop swimming pool, a free movie theatre, sleeping pods, an indoor canyon walk, gardens of orchids, sunflower, cactus and more. Free city tours leave from the airport for travellers on long layovers. The airport processes around 60 million passengers a year. For many people visiting Singapore, the airport itself is one of the country's biggest attractions.

convoQuestions.question: Did you say hello in two different languages today?
Marina Bay, Chinatown, Little India, Sentosa, which has felt the most "Singapore"?
How does Singapore's heat compare to other hot places we have been?
Singapore is the size of a city but it's a whole country. Does that change how it feels?
Singapore invented Singlish, hawker centres, garden skyscrapers, all under 60 years old. How can a young country invent so much?

wordSearch.hint: Singaporean foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Smooth-coated otter
animals.description: Wild otter families have moved back into central Singapore canals and the Marina Bay area. Beloved local celebrities.

animals.name: Asian water monitor
animals.description: Big black lizards up to 2 m long, swimming in canals and crossing park paths. Harmless to humans.

animals.name: Long-tailed macaque
animals.description: In the Bukit Timah forest and MacRitchie reservoir. Keep your snacks zipped up.

animals.name: Tropical butterflies
animals.description: Hundreds of species in the Botanic Gardens and at the Changi Airport butterfly garden.

animals.name: Hornbill
animals.description: Big black birds with bright bills, returning to Singapore parks after decades of absence.

animals.name: Coral reef fish
animals.description: Around the small Singapore Southern Islands (Sisters' Islands, Pulau Hantu).

animals.name: Reticulated python
animals.description: The world's longest snake species lives wild in Singapore parks. Spotted but rarely; harmless if left alone.
